# Forest House Art — Concept Gallery

**Exploratory concept art, not production assets.** 19 isometric house SVGs generated by a
multi-agent swarm as candidates for a "house" element on the forest map (no such element exists yet
in `packages/forest-world-r3f` — every current element there is an undirected placeholder mesh,
ADR-0070: the look is owner-witnessed, never machine-judged). Nothing here has been wired into the
renderer. **Owner's pick is still PENDING** — open `index.html` and look.

## What the swarm was

Four passes, each widening what the agents could actually perceive:

1. **20 generator agents**, each designing one house in its own aesthetic direction (storybook,
   low-poly, fantasy, architectural, nomadic...), as a self-contained isometric SVG on the map's
   `#101418` night-ground palette. One (`floating-isle-hut`) was cut by the owner — not for quality,
   but because a house slung under a floating island would occlude the map beneath it. Not landed here.
2. **Markup critics** read the raw SVG source and scored/critiqued it. This pass was blind to how the
   art actually rendered — it caught style and completeness issues but **missed real geometry
   errors**, which the owner then spotted by eye (floating roofs, an oval "iso" tile that wasn't
   isometric, parts not meeting).
3. **Round-1 geometry pass — the fix for #2's blind spot.** Every house was headless-rendered to a
   PNG on the actual map ground, and a *vision* agent reviewed the picture (not the code), listing
   concrete defects. A second agent fixed only what was flagged. A third, skeptical agent compared
   before/after images and **kept whichever was actually better** — two auto-fixes that introduced a
   new problem were reverted to their originals rather than shipped.
4. **Round-2 artist polish pass — the technique worth reusing.** One senior artist agent per house
   worked in a **render → look → refine loop**: edit the SVG, render its own edit to a PNG, read that
   PNG, fix whatever it (or the original) got wrong, repeat up to three times, and only stop once it
   had looked at and accepted its own output. The same before/after verify judge as #3 ran again
   afterward. All 19 improved versions won their verify. This loop — an agent that can see its own
   rendered work, not just edit blind markup — is the reusable technique. Markup-only review cannot
   catch floating geometry, occlusion errors, or things that don't meet; an agent has to look at a
   picture of the result.

**Totals:** 136 geometry fixes + 78 non-defect polish touches applied across the 19 landed files.

## Strongest candidates

Ranked by how much genuine structural work survived to acceptance, not by taste:

- **`coastal-stilt-house.svg`** — heaviest repair (15 fixes: stilts, bracing, ladder/jetty
  connections) and cleanest resulting silhouette.
- **`canopy-treehouse.svg`** — the round-2 pass caught a real **depth-order inversion** (the trunk
  drew *behind* the deck it was supposed to pierce, with the roots claiming the tile centre while the
  visible trunk vanished); now redrawn with a proper pierced-roof collar.
  **`lowpoly-cabin.svg`** — both flanking trees were floating disconnected in the void off the tile's
  back edge; now seated with contact shadows.
- **`forest-windmill.svg`** — moved off a flat green oval "mound" onto the same 2:1 isometric diamond
  tile convention the rest of the set uses (this was the exact defect the owner flagged by eye before
  round 2 started).
- **`storybook-cottage.svg`** — round 2 found a roof seam gap wide enough that the night background
  showed through as a visible crack, plus a door hanging 15px off its wall plane onto the grass.

## Known defects — read this before deciding fix vs. reference

**No major/structural failures survived round 2** — every remaining item below is agent-flagged
*minor*. But there is real residue, listed per house, blunt on purpose (this file is the input to the
fix-vs-look-reference decision, not a sales pitch):

| House | Remaining defects |
|---|---|
| `canopy-treehouse` | A decorative root/branch swoosh drapes over the deck edge with ambiguous depth order vs. the new railing; the trunk's deck cut-out reads as a slightly-oversized hard-edged parallelogram rather than a tight bark-shaped notch. |
| `coastal-lighthouse` | **Net trade, not a clean win:** fixed a floating lichen blob, a missing gallery railing, a flat-looking chimney, and a pool overhanging the tile edge — but introduced a new occlusion inversion (a relocated rock now draws over an eave tip that should be in front of it) and the tide-pool rim is still tangent to the tile bevel with two foam flecks that read as rivets. |
| `coastal-stilt-house` | The ramp/gangway's railing meets the ground slightly ambiguously — reads a touch detached from its post. |
| `crystal-lumen-house` | A small crystal shard below each flanking spire still floats disconnected from the spire body (unclear if intentional motif or defect — flagged both rounds); the left facade's diamond window straddles a wall panel seam, with the seam line passing visibly through it. |
| `forest-windmill` | The mid-tower balcony band ends in downward dart-spike points that read as torn fabric, not structure; one lower-tower window breaks the arched-opening language of the others; the left sail blade draws behind the balcony railing where it should sweep in front. |
| `hamlet-cluster` | The tall purple house's roof and attic wall merge into one flat navy mass (weak face separation kills the roof-plane read); the small green house's pyramid roof has near-identical values on both visible faces and can misread as concave/hollow at scene scale. |
| `hillside-burrow` | The dug-out doorway recess meets the grass in hard straight vertical seams — reads as a rectangular cutout panel rather than an organic dug entry; the dome's right silhouette terminates in a sharp unnatural cusp. **A round-1 auto-fix attempt on this house's chimney-roof join actually regressed (opened a gap) and was reverted** — the join is original, unfixed geometry. |
| `japanese-minka` | A stepping stone at the plinth's front corner half-overlaps the base fascia instead of sitting on the ground plane; the lantern hanger rods attach in mid-air above the roof with no visible fixing point. |
| `lowpoly-cabin` | The attic diamond window sits cramped against the roof rake fascia post-fix; the front door's stone step is slightly oversized/bright relative to the path stones below it. |
| `modern-glass-cabin` | The deck's front railing steps down abruptly and its low segment ends floating mid-deck with no resolving post — a pre-existing issue neither round touched. |
| `mushroom-dwelling` | Faint radial gill strokes on the cap's underside fade out mid-plane instead of terminating cleanly at the stem (barely visible at ship scale). |
| `nomad-yurt` | The smoke wisp above the cauldron floats slightly disconnected from the fire; the fuel pile beside the fire casts no ground shadow, reading as pasted-on. |
| `rune-stone-hall` | The lower entry-step flagstone is offset from the upper slab — reads as a loose paver, not a connected step. |
| `storybook-cottage` | The chimney flue is two visibly offset slabs joined by a dark angled connector — reads bent/disjointed; the closed roof-seam parting (from the round-2 fix) still tapers awkwardly into the ridge finial — cosmetic residue, no longer see-through. |
| `tiered-pagoda` | Eave shadow arcs slice the tier-2/3 windows into wedges that can misread as "angry eyes" at distance; the base staircase approaches the plinth corner diagonally instead of squaring to the lit doorway. |
| `trail-gatehouse` | **Net trade, not a clean win:** fixed five real faults (exposed wall corner, square-cornered arch interior, flat chimney cap, an unsupported floating jetty beam, a path that ran off the island's front corner) — but the compacted stepping-stone cluster now has one pair z-ordered backwards (farther stone drawn over the nearer one), the left-plinth mushrooms still grow out of a sloped dirt face rather than flat ground, and the new jetty support column's material doesn't match the coursed-stone pier beside it. |
| `tudor-halftimber` | A ground-floor window is almost fully occluded by the jetty beam above it — only a thin yellow sliver visible; a jetty brace slightly overlaps the top-left corner of a window frame on the right facade. |
| `turf-longhouse` | The front door arch is slightly under-sheared for the wall's isometric plane — reads a touch too frontal. **A round-1 auto-fix on the gable's crossed-beam finial and door silhouette regressed and was reverted** — round 2 fixed the finial and door properly; only this minor arch shear remains. |
| `wizard-spire` | The right annex's support corbel is narrower than the bay above it, leaving the bay's outer edge marginally unsupported; the tower's right silhouette has an abrupt tangent break below the annex where the lean bulges — present since round 1, untouched by round 2. |

**Technical hygiene note (not a geometry defect):** `hillside-burrow.svg` and `turf-longhouse.svg`
each contain one internally duplicated element `id` (the same id used twice within that single file —
not a cross-file collision; `index.html`'s inliner namespaces per-file so this doesn't affect this
gallery, but it is invalid SVG and worth a lint pass if these files are ever reused directly).

## Why this matters for what comes next

Two things changed after this swarm started, and this file is written for both of them:

- **`@storytree/procedural-architecture`** is landing: buildings authored as parameters with derived
  positions, mechanically checked for structural coherence. That removes the **floating parts / unmet
  joins** class catalogued above by construction — a parametric system cannot produce a door 15px off
  its wall plane. These SVGs are hand-drawn and will never be fully clean the way a parametric system
  is.

  **Correction (2026-07-19, ADR-0217):** an earlier version of this bullet also claimed the
  **occlusion-inversion** class was removed "by construction". It is not. A part-tree derives
  *positions*; it does not derive *draw order*, and the spike's own documented bug was a centroid
  painter's sort putting a ground-level door behind the wall it was cut into while the checker
  returned zero violations. Depth-order inversion (5 of the 19 houses) needs an **explicit draw-order
  pass** — ADR-0217 station 3, now built as `packages/procedural-architecture/src/draw-order.ts` (a
  BSP that splits interpenetrating polygons, plus `findDepthConflicts` as its oracle). ADR-0214
  decision 1 carried the same wrong claim and was corrected by ADR-0217.
- **A render-backend evaluation is separately in flight.** Once it lands, the owner decides whether to
  (a) hand-fix the defects above and use these as real assets, or (b) treat this gallery purely as
  **look reference** for the new engine (silhouette, palette, mood) while the geometry gets rebuilt
  parametrically. This README exists to make that decision well-informed rather than to argue for
  either path.

## Files

- `*.svg` — 19 house concepts, one file each, self-contained (no external refs, no scripts).
- `index.html` — plain static gallery, all 19 inlined and labelled with pitch + remaining defects.
  Open directly in a browser; no build step, no server, no external requests.
